content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. You need to find the sum of the maximum elements of every nonempty subsequence of the given sequence. As the result may be very large, you should print the value modulo 109+710^9+7109+7 (the remainder when divided by 109+710^9+7109+7).

A subsequence of a given sequence is a sequence that can be derived from the given sequence by deleting some or no elements without changing the order of the remaining elements.


Input Format


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.




The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.




The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.





Output Format

For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the sum of the maximum elements of every nonempty subsequence of the given sequence modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 4·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the sequence. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the sum of the maximum elements of all non‑empty subsequences of the given sequence, modulo 1000000007.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The nonempty subsequences of the given sequence are [1],[2],[3],[1,2],[1,3],[2,3][1],[2],[3],[1,2],[1,3],[2,3][1],[2],[3],[1,2],[1,3],[2,3] and [1,2,3][1,2,3][1,2,3]. Their maximum elements are 1,2,3,2,3,31,2,3,2,3,31,2,3,2,3,3 and 333 respectively. Therefore, the solution is 1+2+3+2+3+3+3=171+2+3+2+3+3+3=171+2+3+2+3+3+3=17.
Example case 2: There are 777 nonempty subsequences that do not contain the number 222 and 888 subsequences that contain the number 222. Therefore, the answer is 7⋅1+8⋅2=237 \cdot 1 + 8 \cdot 2 = 237⋅1+8⋅2=23.
Example case 3: There is only one nonempty subsequence, so the answer is 777.

solution.approach:
For a subsequence its maximum is the rightmost occurrence of the largest value inside it. Therefore each element Ai can be uniquely assigned as the maximum of all subsequences where:
• No element larger than Ai appears in the subsequence.
• Among elements equal to Ai, the chosen occurrence is the rightmost one.
Consequently, for position i we may freely choose any subset of elements before i whose values are ≤ Ai (including equal values) and any subset of elements after i whose values are < Ai. If L_i is the number of indices j < i with Aj ≤ Ai and R_i is the number of indices j > i with Aj < Ai, then the number of subsequences where Ai is the rightmost maximum equals 2^{L_i}·2^{R_i}. The total answer is Σ Ai·2^{L_i+R_i} (mod M).
To obtain L_i and R_i efficiently we process the array twice using a Fenwick tree (BIT) on the compressed values:
1. Compress all Ai to ranks 1…K.
2. Scan from left to right, maintaining a BIT of frequencies of already seen values. For each i, query the prefix sum up to rank(Ai) → L_i, then add Ai to the BIT.
3. Scan from right to left, with a fresh BIT. For each i, query the prefix sum up to rank(Ai)‑1 → R_i (strictly smaller), then add Ai.
Pre‑compute powers of two up to the maximum N (global across test cases) modulo M. The overall complexity per test case is O(N log N) time and O(N) memory.
Edge cases: single element (answer = that element), all equal values (answer = value·(2^N‑1)), decreasing sequence (each element can be followed only by smaller values, formula still works).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case, where N is the length of the sequence. The total over all test cases respects the given limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the compressed array, BITs and power table.
